flashcards.front: What is thread scheduling in operating systems?
flashcards.back: Thread scheduling determines the order and manner in which threads are allocated CPU time. It is the process of deciding which thread runs on the CPU at any given time when multiple threads are competing for execution.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between user-level and kernel-level thread scheduling?
flashcards.back: User-level threads are managed by a thread library at the user space without kernel involvement. Kernel-level threads are managed directly by the operating system kernel. User-level threads are faster to create but cannot utilize multiple CPUs; kernel-level threads can but have higher overhead.

flashcards.front: What are the four main scheduling criteria?
flashcards.back: 1) CPU Utilization - percentage of time CPU is busy. 2) Throughput - number of processes/threads completed per time unit. 3) Turnaround Time - time from submission to completion. 4) Waiting Time - total time a thread spends in ready queue.

flashcards.front: What is response time in thread scheduling?
flashcards.back: Response time is the time from when a thread is submitted (ready state) until it gets CPU time for the first time. It is crucial for interactive systems and determines how quickly the system responds to user requests.

FCFS schedules threads in the order they arrive in the ready queue. The first thread to request CPU gets priority. It is simple but can cause convoy effect where short threads wait behind long ones, leading to poor average waiting time.

flashcards.front: What is SJF (Shortest Job First) scheduling?
flashcards.back: SJF selects the thread with the shortest burst time (execution time) from the ready queue. It minimizes average waiting time but requires knowing or estimating thread lengths in advance. It can cause starvation for long threads.

flashcards.front: How does Round Robin scheduling work?
flashcards.back: Round Robin assigns a fixed time slice (quantum) to each thread in the ready queue. Threads are executed in cyclic order. When a thread's quantum expires, it is moved to the back of the ready queue. It is fair but has overhead from context switches.

flashcards.front: What is priority scheduling in threads?
flashcards.back: Priority scheduling assigns a priority level to each thread. Higher priority threads are executed first. It can be preemptive (higher priority thread preempts running thread) or non-preemptive. May cause starvation of low-priority threads.

Multilevel queue divides the ready queue into multiple separate queues, each with its own scheduling algorithm (e.g., system processes in one queue, interactive in another). Threads are assigned to queues based on their characteristics.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling?
flashcards.back: Preemptive scheduling allows the OS to forcibly remove CPU from a running thread when a higher priority thread arrives. Non-preemptive scheduling lets a thread run to completion or until it voluntarily yields the CPU.

Thread Contention Scope defines the competition for CPU among threads. Process Contention Scope (PCS) is competition among threads within the same process. System Contention Scope (SCS) is competition among all threads in the system for CPU.

Multiple-processor scheduling deals with allocating threads to multiple CPUs. It includes approaches like load balancing, processor affinity (binding threads to specific CPUs), and symmetric multiprocessing (SMP) where all CPUs are equal.

flashcards.front: What is processor affinity in thread scheduling?
flashcards.back: Processor affinity is the ability to bind a thread to a specific CPU or set of CPUs. Soft affinity allows migration but prefers certain CPUs; hard affinity requires a thread to run only on specific CPUs. It improves cache performance.

flashcards.front: What is load balancing in multiprocessor systems?
flashcards.back: Load balancing distributes threads evenly across all processors to maximize utilization. It can be push-based (periodically moving threads from busy to idle CPUs) or pull-based (idle CPUs pull threads from busy ones).

Thread starvation occurs when a thread is perpetually denied CPU access because other threads are continually prioritized over it. This typically happens in priority scheduling where low-priority threads wait indefinitely.
